Comparing Against estdaR and spdyn

Source: vignettes/prior-art-comparison.Rmd
prior-art-comparison.Rmd

estdaR (Vallone et al.) and spdyn (Loaiza) are prior R implementations of spatial distribution dynamics methods. Both predate griddy and remain useful as methodological comparators. griddy does not compete on methodology — it targets a different interface: long sf panels, tidy outputs, and map-ready objects, with the unit identity, time index, class labels, transition labels, and spatial-lag intervals attached to the result.

This vignette shows that griddy::spatial_markov() produces the same transition counts as estdaR::sp.mkv() and spdyn::spMarkov() on a shared panel, and walks through where the conventions intentionally differ.

library(griddy)
library(dplyr)
library(sf)
library(sfdep)
library(spData)
library(spdep)
library(tidyr)

Shared panel

The bundled usjoin panel mirrors PySAL’s reference dataset, so prior R implementations and griddy are working from the same series. griddy consumes weights via the sfdep idiom; estdaR and spdyn consume listw objects from spdep. We construct both from the same neighbor list to keep the comparison fair.

data(usjoin)

geom <- us_states |>
  filter(NAME %in% usjoin$name) |>
  arrange(NAME) |>
  mutate(
    nb = st_contiguity(geometry),
    wt = st_weights(nb)
  )

panel <- usjoin |>
  filter(name %in% geom$NAME) |>
  arrange(name, year)

listw <- nb2listw(geom$nb, glist = geom$wt, style = "W")

spatial <- spatial_markov(panel, name, year, income, geometry = geom, k = 5)

spatial$transitions |>
  select(id, from_time, to_time, lag_class, transition, spatial_lag) |>
  head()
## # A tibble: 6 × 6
##   id      from_time to_time lag_class transition spatial_lag
##   <chr>       <int>   <int> <ord>     <chr>            <dbl>
## 1 Alabama      1929    1930 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          382.
## 2 Alabama      1930    1931 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          326 
## 3 Alabama      1931    1932 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          276.
## 4 Alabama      1932    1933 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          211 
## 5 Alabama      1933    1934 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          207.
## 6 Alabama      1934    1935 Q1        Q1 -> Q1          253.
lag_intervals(spatial)
## # A tibble: 5 × 4
##   class  lower  upper type       
##   <ord>  <dbl>  <dbl> <chr>      
## 1 Q1      181   1077. spatial_lag
## 2 Q2     1077.  2213. spatial_lag
## 3 Q3     2213.  7052. spatial_lag
## 4 Q4     7052. 21042. spatial_lag
## 5 Q5    21042. 52568. spatial_lag
transition_matrix(spatial, "count", lag_class = "Q1")
##     Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4 Q5
## Q1 705 35  0  0  0
## Q2   2 36  0  0  0
## Q3   0  0  0  0  0
## Q4   0  0  0  0  0
## Q5   0  0  0  0  0

Cross-package agreement

The next chunk runs only when estdaR and spdyn are both installed. The package test suite carries static fixtures from both so ordinary CI can validate the spatial Markov counts without depending on either package.

library(estdaR)
library(spdyn)

wide <- panel |>
  pivot_wider(id_cols = name, names_from = year, values_from = income) |>
  arrange(name)

wide_mat <- as.matrix(wide |> select(-name))
year_cols <- as.character(unique(panel$year))

estdar_sp <- estdaR::sp.mkv(wide_mat, listw, classes = 5, fixed = TRUE)
spdyn_sp <- spdyn::spMarkov(
  wide,
  listw,
  stateVars = year_cols,
  n.states = 5,
  pool = TRUE,
  std = FALSE
)

griddy_counts <- aperm(
  array(
    spatial$matrix$n,
    dim = c(5, 5, 5),
    dimnames = list(spatial$states, spatial$states, spatial$lag_states)
  ),
  c(2, 1, 3)
)

all.equal(unname(griddy_counts), unname(estdar_sp$Transitions))
all.equal(unname(griddy_counts), unname(spdyn_sp$t))

When the two prior implementations and griddy agree to within numerical tolerance, all three calls return TRUE. Disagreements with both at once are treated as bugs in griddy. Disagreements with one but not the other are documented under “Conventions worth knowing” below.

Conventions worth knowing

Three places where a naive cross-comparison can produce false disagreement:

  • Classification breaks. griddy uses pooled quantiles by default; estdaR accepts fixed = TRUE/FALSE to choose; spdyn exposes pool. Match those before comparing matrices.
  • Standardization. spdyn::spMarkov(..., std = TRUE) standardizes the panel by year mean before classifying. griddy does not standardize by default. Set std = FALSE for direct comparison.
  • Output shape. estdaR returns a 3D array indexed [from, to, lag]. spdyn returns an object with a $t field of the same shape. griddy returns a long matrix tibble keyed by (lag_class, from_state, to_state), which is what makes downstream dplyr work natural.

The package test suite uses static fixtures from estdaR::sp.mkv() and spdyn::spMarkov() so ordinary CI can validate the spatial Markov counts without installing either package. Live cross-checks run conditionally via skip_if_not_installed().

On this page
